title: The Counting House
text: You sit beside your father in the counting house. Ledgers show cotton bought, cloth sold and wages paid. The mill is noisy, but the accounts are neat.
historyNote: Factory owners used capital, machinery and workers to produce goods for profit. Their decisions shaped the lives of many workers.
evidence: Factory ownership could bring wealth, but it depended on workers, machines and raw materials.

choices.label: Learn how profit is calculated.
Ask how workers' wages are decided.
Walk onto the factory floor.

title: Profit and Wages
text: The accounts show that higher wages reduce profit. Lower wages make workers angry. Your father says a mill must stay competitive to survive.
historyNote: Factory owners faced pressure to control costs. Wages were a major cost, and workers often had little power to demand more.
evidence: Factory profit was linked to low costs, including the wages paid to workers.

choices.label: Argue that wages should stay low to beat competitors.
Suggest fairer wages for skilled workers.
Ask workers what wages mean for their families.

title: The Factory Floor
text: Machines shake the floor. Children carry bobbins and sweep near the frames. You notice how small they look beside the moving belts and rollers.
historyNote: Children worked in many cotton mills. Owners often valued them because they were cheap to employ and could fit around machines.
evidence: Child labour helped keep costs low in some factories, but it exposed children to long hours and danger.

choices.label: Ask why children are working instead of learning.
Focus on how quickly the machines produce cloth.
Watch for dangers on the factory floor.

title: Children at Work
text: A child tells you they start before sunrise and often leave after dark. Their wage helps pay rent, but they are tired all the time.
historyNote: Child labour was tied to poverty. Many families needed children's wages, even when the work was harmful.
evidence: Children's wages supported poor families, which made reform difficult but important.

choices.label: Suggest shorter hours for children.
Decide the family needs their cheap labour.
Ask if school and work could be balanced.

title: Rivals Downriver
text: A rival mill is selling cloth at a lower price. Your family worries that buyers will leave unless your mill becomes cheaper and faster.
historyNote: Competition pushed factory owners to cut costs, invest in machinery and increase output.
evidence: Competition encouraged owners to seek cheaper production, sometimes at workers' expense.

choices.label: Cut costs by keeping wages low.
Improve machinery instead of cutting wages.
Sell better-quality cloth for a fair price.

title: A New Machine
text: An engineer offers a new machine that can produce cloth faster. It costs a great deal, but it may increase profit and beat rival mills.
historyNote: Investment in machinery helped factories produce more goods. It could also reduce the need for some workers.
evidence: Industrial growth relied on investment in machines, buildings and transport.

choices.label: Buy the machine and increase output.
Buy it but retrain workers for new jobs.
Delay the purchase and repair old machines first.

title: Cotton from Across the Empire
text: Bales of raw cotton arrive at the mill. A map in the office shows trade routes across oceans. Your cloth begins with cotton grown far from Britain.
historyNote: British cotton mills depended on global trade and empire. Cotton was connected to colonial power and, in some places, enslaved labour.
evidence: Factory profits in Britain were connected to cotton grown and traded across the world.

choices.label: Trace the cotton route and ask who benefits.
Focus on keeping cotton supplies cheap.
Write a note about empire and the mill's wealth.

title: A Question of Responsibility
text: At dinner, you ask whether the mill should do more for workers. Some relatives say business is business. Others say owners have duties because they hold power.
historyNote: Industrialisation raised moral questions about class, wealth and responsibility. Owners had more power than most workers.
evidence: Factory owners had choices about safety, wages and working conditions.

choices.label: Argue that profit should include worker safety.
Accept that owners should focus on profit.
Suggest a school for child workers.

title: Debating Reform
text: A newspaper reports arguments over factory reform. Reformers want limits on child labour. Some owners say new laws will hurt business and reduce profit.
historyNote: Factory reform led to laws such as the Factory Acts. These laws gradually limited child labour and improved inspection.
evidence: Factory Acts showed that Parliament began to regulate industry because of concerns about workers and children.

choices.label: Support the Factory Acts.
Oppose reform to protect business freedom.
Argue for reform that protects children and supports families.

title: Ending: Wealth and Responsibility
text: Your story shows the choices facing a mill owner's child. Factory ownership could create wealth, cheap cloth and new investment. It also raised hard questions about wages, child labour, empire, competition and responsibility.
historyNote: The Mill Owner's Child pathway shows how class shaped the Industrial Revolution. Owners had more choices and power than most workers, so their decisions mattered.
evidence: Industrial wealth brought responsibility because profit often depended on workers, children and global cotton supplies.
